title: Brooding chicks from hatch to outdoor transfer
subtitle: The heat lamp routine, the temperature steps, and the moment to move chicks outside.
excerpt: The critical window for chicks reared without a broody hen is the first six weeks. Get the heat lamp height right, drop the temperature steadily, and resist moving them outdoors until feathering is complete. Most losses in artificially brooded chicks come from chilling in the first days or moving them outside too early.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Chickens Reared for Meat (2018), gov.uk. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive: artificial rearing chapter. Merck Veterinary Manual: chick brooding management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A warm enclosure for newly hatched chicks being raised without a hen. The central heat source is typically a red heat lamp suspended above a bedded pen. The brooder area provides a warm zone the chick can move toward or away from to regulate its own temperature.

A low cardboard or hardboard surround placed in a circle around the heat lamp for the first two to three days, keeping chicks close to warmth and food. Without a ring, newly hatched chicks wander away from the heat source and chill.
A female chicken in her first year of lay, from the point at which she starts laying until her first birthday.
A fine-particle starter feed for newly hatched chicks, typically containing 20 to 22% protein to support rapid early growth. Fed for the first four weeks before moving to grower pellets.

content.text: Artificial brooding replaces what a hen does naturally for the first six weeks of a chick's life: providing warmth on demand. The
brooder
does not need to be elaborate. A solid-sided pen with a bedded floor, a red heat lamp on a secure adjustable fitting, chick drinkers and feeders, and a
brooding ring
for the first few days is everything needed.
Setting up before the chicks arrive

Run the heat lamp for at least two hours before chicks arrive to warm the floor. The floor temperature directly under the lamp should be 32 to 35 C in the first week; the ambient temperature at the edge of the brooding area should be cooler, around 24 to 26 C, so chicks can self-regulate. A thermometer on the floor is worth having for the first few days.

Lay several sheets of paper towel over the wood shavings bedding on day one. Chicks need to eat immediately but cannot always distinguish shavings from food; paper towel over the surface for the first 24 to 48 hours, with chick crumb scattered on top, prevents them filling up on bedding.
Reading the chicks' behaviour

attrs.title: What the chick distribution tells you

content.text: Chicks huddled in a tight mass under the lamp: too cold. Raise the lamp or add a second lamp. Chicks spread to the outer edges, backs to the heat source: too hot. Raise the lamp further. Chicks spread evenly across the pen, some resting, some eating, some drinking: the temperature is correct. A steady background of gentle cheeping is the sound of a comfortable brooder; persistent loud distress cheeping in a cluster signals cold.
The temperature reduction schedule

Raise the lamp by a few centimetres each week to reduce the temperature by roughly 3 C per week. By the end of week four the chicks should be comfortable at around 20 C with the lamp still available at night. By week six, if the ambient temperature of the building is consistent, many chicks will stop using the lamp altogether and it can be switched off during the day.
Feed and water management
Feed
chick crumb

from day one until four weeks. Move to grower pellets at week four. Ensure fresh water is available at all times; small chick drinkers with shallow channels prevent drowning. Raise drinker height to shoulder height as the chicks grow, which reduces contamination from scratching.
Moving outside

Chicks are ready to move to an outdoor run when they are fully feathered, usually at five to six weeks for most laying breeds. Check by parting the feathers on the back and chest: if down is visible, they are not ready. Introduce them to the outdoor pen on a warm, still day. Provide a covered shelter they can retreat to; chicks that have only known artificial brooding will initially avoid open sky.
The transition from brooder to outdoor life is covered in more detail in
raising chicks to point of lay

attrs.label: raising chicks to point of lay

content.text: . The
pullet
will reach laying age at 18 to 22 weeks depending on breed.
Chicks found dead in a cluster at day 1 or 2
Chilling, either from inadequate lamp temperature or from the lamp failing overnight. Death by dehydration can also occur if chicks cannot find water.
Check the lamp is working and measure floor temperature before the next batch. On day one, dip each chick's beak gently into the drinker to ensure it has drunk.
Feather pecking beginning at week 2 or 3
Overcrowding, excessive light intensity, or insufficient feed access points.
Increase floor space, switch to a dimmer red lamp, and add additional feeders. Early feather pecking becomes habitual quickly; address it in the first 48 hours of observation.
